essential_question: Does a triangle built from string at exactly 3, 4, and 5 units always close into a real right angle — and does a² + b² = c² hold up when you measure the real world?
objective: Build a 3-4-5 right triangle from string and confirm it forms a true right angle, then measure a real right angle (a ramp, a door brace, a corner) and check that its three side lengths satisfy a² + b² = c² within measurement tolerance.

agenda.title: Brief — From Numbers to the Real World

content.heading: From the equation on paper to a right angle you can check
bullets: Yesterday: stated a² + b² = c² and used it to find missing sides, keeping radicals simplified.
Today: build a real 3-4-5 triangle from string and confirm it truly makes a right angle, then test the rule against a right angle you measure in real life.
The test: does the math predict the angle before you ever check it with a square?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather string or twine, a tape measure or ruler, tape or a marker for knots, and a protractor or carpenter's square. Decide where you'll mark 3, 4, and 5 units along your string, and pick one real right angle around your home to measure afterward.

content.heading: String triangle, then a real-world check
bullets: String Triangle — mark your string (or a length of rope) at 3, 4, and 5 equal units, tie it into a loop, and pull it taut at three points to form a triangle.
Check the angle where the 3-unit and 4-unit sides meet with a protractor or a carpenter's square — is it 90°?
Real Right Angle — find one real right angle around your home (a ramp, a door-frame brace, a ladder against a wall, a corner shelf bracket) and measure its two legs and its hypotenuse.
Compute a² + b² and c² for your real measurement and compare.

content.preview: Session 3: defend why a² + b² = c² actually works and use the converse to test if a triangle is right, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can build a 3-4-5 triangle from string and confirm it forms a true right angle
I can measure a real right angle's legs and hypotenuse and check that a² + b² = c² within measurement tolerance
I can identify which of three measured lengths must be the hypotenuse before doing any math

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W20 The Pythagorean Theorem.md

questions.prompt: Build a 3-4-5 triangle from string or rope marked at 3, 4, and 5 equal units, pull it taut into a triangle, and measure the angle where the 3-unit and 4-unit sides meet with a protractor or carpenter's square. Record the angle.
questions.explain: Because 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25 = 5², the converse of the Pythagorean theorem guarantees this triangle must be a right triangle — your measured angle should land at (or very near) 90°.

questions.prompt: Measure the two legs and the hypotenuse of a real right angle around your home. Record all three lengths, then compute a² + b² and c² and compare.
questions.answer: Any honestly measured trio where a² + b² comes out close to c² (allowing for ruler and rounding error), e.g. legs 6 in and 8 in, hypotenuse ≈10 in → 36 + 64 = 100 = 10².
questions.explain: Rubric: three measured lengths recorded (1 pt) + a² + b² computed and compared to c², with a reasonable real-world match (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Before doing any math, which of your three measured lengths did you expect to be the hypotenuse, and how did you know?
questions.answer: The longest of the three, and the one opposite the right angle.
questions.explain: The hypotenuse is always the longest side of a right triangle and always sits opposite the 90° angle — you can spot it before any computation.

questions.prompt: Reflect: how close did your real-world measurement come to a² + b² = c² exactly, and what do you think caused any gap — the theorem, or your ruler?
